> Fork JEP and remove the checks in Jep.isValidThread() to return always true. I have done some work with JNI already and would not confirm that multithreading and JNI in general is a problem (as the JEP doc suggessts). This however is the most dirty solution...
1. JNI is not the limit on threading, Python is. Python internally has thread local variables tracking the PyThreadState and if you don't handle this exactly right you will crash, so you would need significant changes to the Jep data structure and interaction with the python library to make this work, if it is even possible.
> Wrap the single instance into a threaded on it's own and talk to it using a wrapper and RMI internally. Would work, but still somwhow crude.
2. I believe this is the most common approach, I'm not sure what you had in mind but you can easily integrate basic java multithreading datastructures like Queues, Callables and Futures to pass the data between threads.
> I read something about the "shared modules feature", which could solve the problem somehow on Python level. But I was not able to find an example or more description of it, so don't know how to use this. However, just from reading some basic information about it this could be a nice fit as the "one global Python lib instance" is exactly what we require and I could still guarantee that only a single thread will access the JEP environment.
3. Using shared modules should work. It was not really designed for this use case so I don't know of anyone else using it this way but there is no technical reason it shouldn't work. You would just need to put everything you are sharing in a python module and set your JepConfig to share that module and then any classes, functions, and objects within that module would be shared. For some libraries it can be complicated getting shared modules working so if you are using Jep 3.8 you might consider a [SharedInterpreter](http://ninia.github.io/jep/javadoc/3.8/jep/SharedInterpreter.html) instead of Jep with shared modules.

@bddppq regarding why you need topological sort, imagine you have an identity op ID that just passes through its input, and a graph that looks like

input_x -> ID_1 -> intermediate_y -> ID_2 -> output_z

Assume that only the shape of input_x is provided initially.

shape inference must be applied to ID_1 before it's applied to ID_2, so that shape inference for ID_2 knows the shape of intermediate_y.

Here is my understanding: I believe we want the model to be "statically typable" (using the type information supplied in the model plus type-inference and shape-inference). But we haven't yet fully formalized what this means. As @linkerzhang says, we require the model to specify the types of graph inputs/outputs, which includes shapes of (input/output) tensors. However, shapes are permitted to be symbolic identifiers (representing statically unknown values). The expectation is that such symbolic identifiers are used only when the corresponding operators permit them (that is, are polymorphic with respect to this particular dimension size). To capture this, we need to capture the type-signatures of operators, including shape-information, which we haven't so far.

Adding the IDisposable interface would make it a breaking change, which is something we absolutely do not want. Adding an abstract method would also make it a breaking change. In these 2 situations the user _has to_ implement a new termination method. This is undesirable.

We want something where the user does not have to add anything should they not wish to add de-initialising code, but they could add it if they wanted to. It would be best to make the method called on bot termination overrideable instead of abstract or IDisposable. I suggest making a method `void OnBotRetire()` in the `Bot` class, which gets called when the bot gets terminated. It would get called at the top of the `BotManager.StopBotProcess` method: https://github.com/RLBot/RLBot/blob/master/src/main/cs/RLBotDotNet/RLBotDotNet/Manager/BotManager.cs#L126.
